properties.name: Alabama Hills
properties.hook: A field of rounded orange boulders sits at the foot of the highest mountain in the Lower 48, and Hollywood has been filming here for a hundred years.

properties.name: Alamere Falls
properties.hook: A waterfall that pours off a cliff straight onto the sand, at the end of a long, unmarked, slightly dangerous hike almost no casual visitor finishes.

properties.name: The Albany Bulb
properties.hook: A construction landfill that turned into an unofficial sculpture park, out on a spit in the bay.

properties.name: Alcatraz Island
properties.hook: The most famous prison in America sits on an island in the bay, and its fullest story isn't the cellblock but the nineteen months a group of Native activists held it.

properties.name: Alice's Restaurant
properties.hook: The wooden roadhouse where Skyline meets Highway 84, and every motorcycle and road bike in the Bay Area seems to end up on a weekend.

properties.name: Colonel Allensworth State Historic Park
properties.hook: The first California town founded, financed, and governed by Black Americans stands restored on the valley floor — schoolhouse, church, and library rebuilt exactly where Colonel Allensworth planted them in 1908.

properties.name: Alpine Inn (Rossotti's)
properties.hook: An 1852 roadhouse beer garden, second-oldest tavern in California, that also happens to be where the internet took its first real breath.

properties.name: Alum Rock Park
properties.hook: California's oldest municipal park hides the stone ruins of a Victorian health spa — mineral-spring grottoes still seeping sulfur water in a canyon above San Jose.

properties.name: The American River Parkway
properties.hook: A paved trail runs 32 unbroken miles up a river through the middle of Sacramento, one of the longest purpose-built bike paths in the country, and locals call it the city's gem.

properties.name: Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest
properties.hook: A grove of trees near the top of the White Mountains has been alive since before the pyramids, and the Forest Service won't tell you which one is the oldest.

properties.name: Andrew Molera State Park
properties.hook: The big Big Sur park with no scenic drive-in, so the crowds thin the second you're out of the parking lot and across the river.

properties.name: The Android Lawn Statues
properties.hook: A lawn of giant foam mascots at the Googleplex, one for every dessert-named Android release, from a Cupcake to a two-story robot.

properties.name: Angel Island
properties.hook: An island in the middle of the bay with a 360-degree view of the whole region and a haunting immigration station where a century of arrivals were detained.

properties.name: Angora Lakes
properties.hook: Up a one-lane road above Fallen Leaf Lake, two small lakes sit in a granite bowl, and the resort at the top has been selling the same lemonade for a century.

properties.name: Año Nuevo
properties.hook: A windswept point where thousands of elephant seals haul out to fight, breed, and molt, reachable in winter only on foot with a docent.

properties.name: Anza-Borrego & the Desert Sculptures
properties.hook: The largest state park in the country runs on badlands and slot canyons and a wildflower bloom that only shows up some springs, and someone scattered a hundred and thirty giant metal dinosaurs across the desert floor.

properties.name: Apple Jack's
properties.hook: An 1870s dive bar in the La Honda redwoods, dark and creaky and unbothered, that's been watering loggers, bikers, hippies, and cyclists for 150 years.

properties.name: Arcata Marsh & Wildlife Sanctuary
properties.hook: The city of Arcata treats its sewage by running it through a marsh, and that marsh is now one of the best places to watch birds on the whole North Coast.

properties.name: Arizona Cactus Garden
properties.hook: A hidden Victorian desert garden on the Stanford campus, a thousand cacti planted for the Stanfords in the 1880s, free and almost always empty.

properties.name: Armstrong Redwoods
properties.hook: Wine country's old-growth grove: a 1,400-year-old giant named for the lumberman who couldn't bring himself to cut it, in a cool canyon a mile from Guerneville's river scene.

properties.name: Auburn Confluence & Foresthill Bridge
properties.hook: Two forks of the American River meet in a canyon below the tallest bridge in California — 730 feet of steel built for a reservoir that never came, over water that still runs wild.

properties.name: Avenue of the Giants
properties.hook: The old highway was left in place when the freeway bypassed it — 31 miles of two-lane road threading through the largest remaining old-growth redwood forest on Earth.

properties.name: Baker Beach
properties.hook: The classic Golden Gate view from the sand, a disappearing cannon in the dunes behind it, and the beach where Burning Man started with twenty people and an eight-foot wooden man.

properties.name: The Donkeys of Barron Park
properties.hook: Two donkeys in a Palo Alto pasture, one of whom modeled for Shrek's Donkey.

properties.name: The Bay Model
properties.hook: A working scale model of the entire San Francisco Bay, the size of two football fields, with tides that cycle every fourteen minutes.

properties.name: Bean Hollow & Pebble Beach
properties.hook: A beach of tiny polished pebbles and cliffs eaten into honeycomb, a short walk apart, that nearly everyone drives past.

properties.name: Benicia
properties.hook: California's forgotten third capital keeps its 1852 capitol building intact, a waterfront main street pointed at the strait — and a Civil War arsenal whose "camel barns" once auctioned off the US Army's camels.

properties.name: Bernal Heights Summit
properties.hook: A bare grassy hill in the middle of San Francisco with a full 360° of the city — downtown, both bridges, the bay, San Bruno Mountain — and, when the city hasn't taken it down yet, a rope swing hung from the one tree, out over the whole view.

properties.name: The Glory Hole (Lake Berryessa)
properties.hook: When Lake Berryessa fills past the brim, the overflow doesn't run over a dam. It vanishes down a 72-foot hole in the middle of the water.

properties.name: Big Basin Redwoods
properties.hook: California's first state park — the 1902 victory that started redwood conservation — burned almost entirely in 2020, and visiting now means watching a 2,000-year-old forest rebuild itself in real time.

properties.name: Big Pine Lakes
properties.hook: A chain of impossibly turquoise lakes sits below the southernmost glacier in the country, colored by the rock the ice grinds to powder.

properties.name: Bixby Creek Bridge
properties.hook: The most photographed thing on the coast, and the clearest example of what this map is trying to steer you around.

properties.name: Black Chasm Cavern
properties.hook: A Gold Country cave holds more than ten thousand helictites — crystals that ignore gravity, twisting sideways and up out of the walls like frozen smoke.

properties.name: Black Diamond Mines
properties.hook: Northern California's largest coal mining district ran in these hills above Antioch — five vanished towns, a hilltop pioneer cemetery, and a sand mine you can walk 950 feet into on a lantern-lit tour.

properties.name: Black Star Canyon
properties.hook: A remote canyon in the Santa Ana Mountains draws hikers with a hidden waterfall and a dark reputation, though the story behind the reputation may not be true.

properties.name: Bodega Head & the Hole in the Head
properties.hook: A granite headland that rode up the San Andreas Fault from hundreds of miles south — with cliffs among the best gray-whale perches on the coast, and a pond that's really the abandoned excavation for a nuclear plant nobody dared build.

properties.name: Bodega (The Birds)
properties.hook: The schoolhouse and church from Hitchcock's "The Birds," still standing in a sleepy inland town that looks eerily unchanged sixty years on.

properties.name: Bodie
properties.hook: The best-preserved ghost town in the American West sits at 8,400 feet east of the Sierra — 200 buildings frozen mid-abandonment, interiors still set with bottles, desks, and a roulette wheel.

properties.name: Bolinas
properties.hook: The coastal town so committed to being left alone that residents kept tearing down the highway sign pointing to it.

properties.name: Bon Tempe & the Mt Tam Lakes
properties.hook: Mount Tamalpais hides a chain of five reservoir lakes on its north flank — pine-ringed, trout-stocked, eagle-visited — that even many Marin residents haven't found.

properties.name: Boonville & the Boontling Language
properties.hook: An isolated valley town invented its own language in the 1890s, some 1,600 words of it, and spent the next century speaking a tongue nobody outside could follow.

properties.name: Bowling Ball Beach
properties.hook: At the right low tide, a Mendocino beach reveals rows of near-perfect stone spheres — two to five feet across — lined up in the sandstone reef like a giant's abandoned bowling alley.

properties.name: The Broderick-Terry Duel Site
properties.hook: Two stone pillars ten paces apart in a Daly City park mark the last great American duel, where a sitting US senator was shot dead in 1859.

properties.name: Brooks Island
properties.hook: A whole island in the middle of the bay — 2,500-year-old Ohlone shellmound, a 160-foot summit, and Bing Crosby's old private hunting club — closed to everyone except a handful of naturalist-led paddle tours a year.

properties.name: Bubblegum Alley
properties.hook: A downtown alley in San Luis Obispo has both walls packed with decades of chewed gum, fifteen feet high and seventy feet long.

properties.name: Buck's of Woodside
properties.hook: The wildly kitschy Woodside diner where some of Silicon Valley's biggest deals got signed over pancakes, under a Soviet spacesuit and a giant Statue of Liberty arm.

properties.name: Buena Vista Park's Tombstone Gutters
properties.hook: The rain gutters of San Francisco's oldest park are lined with broken Gold Rush-era tombstones — the leftovers from when the city evicted its dead.

properties.name: McArthur-Burney Falls
properties.hook: A 129-foot waterfall that doesn't come from the creek above it — most of the water bursts straight out of the rock face from underground springs, so it pours at the same rate in a drought August as it does in May.

properties.name: Butano State Park
properties.hook: A whole redwood canyon folded into the hills behind Pescadero that most coast traffic drives straight past — the quiet alternative to every famous grove in the region.

properties.name: Byxbee Park
properties.hook: A capped garbage dump turned into a field of earth art: hundreds of leaning poles, chevrons aimed at the runway, a wind-shaped hill, all built on methane.

properties.name: California State Railroad Museum
properties.hook: Twenty-one lavishly restored locomotives and cars — some dating to 1863 — fill a cavernous hall in Old Sacramento, in what's widely called the finest railroad museum in North America, on the ground where the transcontinental railroad actually began.

properties.name: Calaveras Big Trees
properties.hook: The grove that introduced the world to giant sequoias — and promptly cut the biggest one down and turned its stump into a dance floor, an outrage that helped invent conservation.

properties.name: The Camera Obscura
properties.hook: A building shaped like a giant camera perches on the cliff by the old Cliff House — and inside, a rotating lens quietly projects a live, moving 360° image of the crashing ocean onto a dish in a dark room, exactly as it has since 1946.

properties.name: Carmel-by-the-Sea
properties.hook: A one-square-mile village of storybook cottages with no street numbers and a white-sand beach at the bottom of the main street.

properties.name: Carmel Mission (San Carlos Borromeo)
properties.hook: The stone basilica in Carmel was the headquarters of the entire California mission chain — and Junípero Serra, who ran all of it from here, is buried under the altar floor where he died in 1784.

properties.name: Carquinez Scenic Drive
properties.hook: The old bluff-top road between Crockett and Martinez was too landslide-prone to keep as a through road — so the middle became a car-free ribbon above the strait, and the whole thing became the East Bay's best slow mile.

properties.name: Carrizo Plain
properties.hook: The last great piece of the grassland sea that once filled the Central Valley — where superblooms turn fifty miles of plain solid gold, and the San Andreas Fault shows itself on the surface as clearly as anywhere on Earth.

properties.name: Castello di Amorosa
properties.hook: One man spent $40 million and fifteen years building an authentically medieval Tuscan castle in Calistoga — 107 rooms, a moat, a drawbridge, and a real dungeon with a genuine late-Renaissance iron maiden — that happens to sell wine.

properties.name: Castle Air Museum
properties.hook: Eighty-odd military aircraft sit out on a field in the Central Valley, an SR-71 and a B-52 among them, on the old bomber base that once flew them.

properties.name: Castle Rock State Park
properties.hook: High on the Santa Cruz Mountains' crest, wind and rain have hollowed the sandstone into honeycombed caves and bulbous formations that look like sleeping stone giants — a natural climbing gym where a young Chris Sharma learned the sport.

properties.name: Cataract Falls
properties.hook: After winter rain, a creek off Mount Tamalpais tumbles down a mossy fern-walled staircase in waterfall after waterfall — the most beloved cascade hike in Marin, and it looks like a rainforest most people don't know California has.

properties.name: César E. Chávez National Monument
properties.hook: The farm-labor leader who changed California is buried in a rose garden at the union headquarters he built in the Tehachapi foothills.

properties.name: Chabot Space & Science Center
properties.hook: Three historic telescopes in the Oakland hills — including a 36-inch reflector — open to the public for free every clear Friday and Saturday night, staffed by volunteers who want you to see Saturn.

properties.name: Channel Islands National Park
properties.hook: Take a boat out from Ventura to a chain of wild islands where a fox the size of a house cat lives and nothing else on Earth does, and paddle into one of the biggest sea caves on the continent.

properties.name: China Camp
properties.hook: A 19th-century Chinese shrimp-fishing village survives whole on a quiet San Rafael cove — pier, cabins, and all — because one man, born there in 1925, stayed his entire life.

properties.name: Chinatown (the alleys)
properties.hook: The oldest Chinatown in the country, once you get off the souvenir street and into the alleys where it actually lives.

properties.name: City of Ten Thousand Buddhas
properties.hook: One of the largest Buddhist monastic communities in the Western Hemisphere occupies a former state mental hospital outside Ukiah — a hall lined floor to ceiling with ten thousand Buddha statues, and peacocks patrolling the grounds.

properties.name: Clear Lake & Mount Konocti
properties.hook: The oldest lake in North America — holding water continuously for something like half a million years — sits two hours north of the bay under a dormant volcano, and almost nobody from the Bay Area has been.

properties.name: Clouds Rest
properties.hook: The best summit view in Yosemite isn't Half Dome — it's the peak a thousand feet above it, with no cables, no lottery, and a final ridge just narrow enough to make you earn it.

properties.name: Codornices Slide & the Rose Garden
properties.hook: Across the street from each other in the Berkeley hills: a WPA rose amphitheater with a Golden Gate view, and a 40-foot concrete slide that adults ride on flattened cardboard with zero shame.

properties.name: Colma
properties.hook: A town where the dead outnumber the living by more than a thousand to one, built entirely to bury San Francisco's.

properties.name: Coloma & the Gold Discovery
properties.hook: The exact spot where California began: James Marshall's sawmill tailrace on the American River, where a glint in the water on January 24, 1848 set off the largest migration in the hemisphere's history.

properties.name: Columbia State Historic Park
properties.hook: A whole gold-rush town survives with no cars in it, costumed shopkeepers working the same trades, and a stagecoach still running the main street.

properties.name: Computer History Museum
properties.hook: The world's biggest collection of old computers, including a hand-cranked Victorian calculating engine and the actual teapot that shaped all of 3D graphics.

properties.name: Convict Lake
properties.hook: A glacial lake pinned against a 12,000-foot wall of banded rock, named for an 1871 shootout where escaped Carson City convicts killed a posse leader on its shore.

properties.name: Corona Heights
properties.hook: A bare red-rock crown above the Castro with a full downtown panorama — and on its flank, a wall of chert polished to a mirror by the fault that shaped it, one of the best "slickensides" geologists can show you anywhere.

properties.name: Cosumnes River Preserve
properties.hook: The last major undammed river off the Sierra ends here in a sprawl of winter wetlands — and every November thousands of sandhill cranes drop out of the dusk to roost, bugling like something from the Pleistocene.

properties.name: Cowell-Purisima Trail
properties.hook: A flat bluff-top trail south of Half Moon Bay that runs the cliff edge past working farm fields to a hidden pocket beach, and stays quiet while the town beaches fill.

properties.name: Coyote Hills
properties.hook: A knot of grassy hills on the Fremont bayshore where 2,000-year-old Ohlone shellmounds meet tule marshes — and one of the best sunset panoramas on the entire bay.

properties.name: Coyote Point & CuriOdyssey
properties.hook: A eucalyptus-covered point jutting into the bay with a beach, a stretch of the Bay Trail, and a little wildlife center full of rescued native animals.

properties.name: Crocker Art Museum
properties.hook: The oldest art museum in the West sits in a Sacramento railroad baron's 1872 mansion, gallery upstairs and a bowling alley down.

properties.name: Crystal Cove
properties.hook: A colony of forty-six hand-built 1930s beach cottages survives on an Orange County cove, a rustic slice of old California beach life that the coast paved over everywhere else.

properties.name: Cypress Tree Tunnel & KPH Radio
properties.hook: A quarter-mile corridor of Monterey cypress, planted around 1930, leads to a white Art Deco radio station that took down the first West Coast word of Pearl Harbor — and still taps out Morse code today.

properties.name: Deetjen's Big Sur Inn
properties.hook: A ramshackle Norwegian village of hand-built redwood cabins with thin walls, no TVs, and a candlelit dining room that's been running since 1939.

properties.name: Del Valle
properties.hook: A five-mile lake folded into the golden hills south of Livermore — the East Bay's best swimming water, and the back door into its biggest wilderness.

properties.name: The Delta Levee Loop (Hwy 160)
properties.hook: Highway 160 rides the top of the Sacramento River levees for forty miles — eye-level with boat traffic, above the sunken islands, through drawbridges and towns the twentieth century forgot to update.

properties.name: Desolation Wilderness
properties.hook: West of Tahoe the forest gives out and the land turns to bare white granite holding more than a hundred alpine lakes.

properties.name: Devils Postpile & Rainbow Falls
properties.hook: A cliff of basalt columns so geometrically perfect it looks stacked by hand — 60-foot hexagonal posts a glacier sheared off and polished flat on top, so you can walk out onto the columns' honeycombed ends like paving stones.

properties.name: Devil's Slide Trail
properties.hook: A stretch of old Highway 1 so treacherous they finally drilled a tunnel around it, now a flat cliff-edge trail you get to walk or ride with no cars.

properties.name: di Rosa Center for Contemporary Art
properties.hook: A former vineyard turned into 200 acres of contemporary art, with sculpture out in the meadows, galleries by a lake, and peacocks strutting the grounds.

properties.name: Djerassi Sculpture Ranch
properties.hook: 580 acres of Woodside ridge scattered with decades of art, an artists' retreat founded by the man who co-invented the birth-control pill.

properties.name: Donner Memorial State Park
properties.hook: A stone monument stands with its pedestal exactly twenty-two feet tall, the depth of the snow that trapped the Donner Party here in the winter of 1846.

properties.name: The Donner Summit Railroad Tunnels
properties.hook: You can walk straight through the abandoned summit tunnel that Chinese laborers cut through Sierra granite by hand for the first transcontinental railroad.

properties.name: Drawbridge (ghost town)
properties.hook: The Bay Area has an actual ghost town: a former hunting-resort village in the South Bay marshes, abandoned since 1979, whose weathered cabins are slowly sinking into the mud.

properties.name: East Brother Light Station
properties.hook: A working 1874 Victorian lighthouse on its own tiny island in San Pablo Bay — run as a five-room inn where the innkeepers boat you out, cook you dinner, and fire up the original foghorn.

properties.name: Edgewood Park
properties.hook: A serpentine grassland that erupts into one of the Bay Area's best wildflower shows each spring, hidden between the freeways of Redwood City.

properties.name: El Corte de Madera & the Tafoni Monolith
properties.hook: A redwood mountain-bike preserve on Skyline hiding a sandstone monolith that weather carved into a giant honeycomb skull.

properties.name: El Palo Alto
properties.hook: The thousand-year-old redwood the whole town is named after, standing alone by the railroad tracks.

properties.name: Elkhorn Slough
properties.hook: Trade the coast drive for a morning and paddle a tidal slough that holds the largest raft of sea otters on the planet.

properties.name: Emerald Bay via the Rubicon Trail
properties.hook: Tahoe's postcard — a green-glass bay with a stone Scandinavian castle and the lake's only island — earns its fame; the trick is arriving along the shoreline instead of fighting for the overlook.

properties.name: Esalen Hot Springs
properties.hook: Cliffside hot springs over the open Pacific, open to outsiders for exactly two hours in the dead of night.

properties.name: Ewoldsen Trail
properties.hook: The actual hike in the park everyone drives to for a ten-minute waterfall photo, and the one that earns the trip.

properties.name: Fallen Leaf Lake
properties.hook: A mile from the South Lake Tahoe sprawl hides a glacial lake that's basically Tahoe in miniature — warmer, quieter, ringed by Desolation's peaks — that tourists drive past without a glance.

properties.name: Farallon Islands (by boat)
properties.hook: Twenty-seven miles off the Golden Gate stand the "Devil's Teeth" — the wildest place in the Bay Area, home to the largest US seabird colony south of Alaska, patrolled by great whites, visitable only by a day-long boat pilgrimage.

properties.name: Feather Falls
properties.hook: A 410-foot waterfall pours off a sheer cliff in the Sierra foothills above Lake Oroville, and a viewing platform hangs on the canyon rim directly across from it.

properties.name: Fern Canyon & Gold Bluffs Beach
properties.hook: A creek runs down a canyon whose walls stand fifty feet high and solid green with ferns, some from plant families older than the dinosaurs.

properties.name: Ferndale & the Kinetic Race
properties.hook: A whole town of ornate Victorian "butterfat palaces" survives on the Humboldt coast, and every spring it's where a three-day race of giant human-powered art sculptures crosses the finish line.

properties.name: The Filbert Steps
properties.hook: Where Filbert Street hits Telegraph Hill it gives up on being a street entirely: 400-odd steps through private gardens, wooden cottages from the 1870s, and the famous flock of wild parrots.

properties.name: Filoli
properties.hook: A 1917 Georgian country estate and sixteen acres of formal gardens in the Woodside hills, so photogenic it played the mansion in Dynasty's opening credits.

properties.name: Fitzgerald Marine Reserve
properties.hook: One of the richest tide-pool reefs in California, where a low tide turns a mile of rock into a live aquarium.

properties.name: The Flintstone House
properties.hook: The bulbous orange house off Interstate 280 that everyone points at, and that got its owner sued over the dinosaurs in the yard.

properties.name: Forestiere Underground Gardens
properties.hook: A Sicilian immigrant spent forty years hand-digging ten acres of rooms, courtyards, and grottoes beneath Fresno — with citrus trees growing underground, reaching up through skylights he carved for them.

properties.name: Fort Funston
properties.hook: 200-foot sand bluffs at San Francisco's southwest corner where hang gliders launch off old coastal-defense batteries and half the city's dogs run leash-free below.

properties.name: Fort Ord National Monument
properties.hook: An army base where soldiers trained for three wars is now 86 miles of trail across the hills above Monterey Bay.

properties.name: Fort Point
properties.hook: A Civil War fort tucked directly under the Golden Gate Bridge, with the great arch overhead built specifically so the fort could stay.

properties.name: Fort Ross
properties.hook: A wooden Russian fort with an onion-domed chapel on the wild Sonoma coast, the southern edge of the Russian empire in America.

properties.name: Foster's Bighorn
properties.hook: In a Delta river town, you can eat dinner in a 1931 bar under the mounted heads of some three hundred big-game animals, one of them an elephant with a thirteen-foot trunk.

properties.name: Frenchman's Tower
properties.hook: A lonely two-story brick tower in the Palo Alto foothills that nobody can quite explain, so everyone made up a story.

properties.name: Garrapata State Park
properties.hook: No sign, no entrance, no lot. Just numbered gates on the shoulder of Highway 1 and some of the best coast north of Big Sur proper.

properties.name: General Sherman & the Giant Forest
properties.hook: The largest living thing on Earth by volume is a giant sequoia in the southern Sierra, and it stands in a forest full of trees nearly its size.

properties.name: Ghost Railroad of the Santa Cruz Mountains
properties.hook: A mainline railroad once tunneled straight through the Santa Cruz Mountains — mile-long bores, resort stops, whole towns — and the forest has eaten nearly all of it. The portals are still out there.

properties.name: The Giant Dipper (Santa Cruz Boardwalk)
properties.hook: A 1924 wooden roller coaster — a National Historic Landmark still running its original half-mile of lumber — anchors the last old-school seaside amusement park on the West Coast.

properties.name: Glen Canyon Park
properties.hook: An actual wild canyon in the middle of San Francisco — chert cliffs, a free-flowing creek, and resident coyotes — that a shocking number of locals have never heard of.

properties.name: Goat Rock & the Russian River Mouth
properties.hook: The dramatic mouth of the Russian River, where a colony of harbor seals hauls out on the sand between the river and the sea.

properties.name: Golden Gate Park
properties.hook: A thousand acres of made land, bigger than Central Park, holding the oldest Japanese garden in the country, a Victorian glass conservatory, and a paddock of bison.

properties.name: Andy Goldsworthy in the Presidio
properties.hook: The largest collection of Andy Goldsworthy's land art in North America, hidden in a forest you have to walk to find.

properties.name: The Great Spirit Path
properties.hook: A poem laid into the ground across a bayfront park, its verses marked by pictograph sculptures, hidden on a capped landfill.

properties.name: Grizzly Peak & "The Wall"
properties.hook: The pullouts along Grizzly Peak Boulevard serve the East Bay's definitive sunset view — and the one locals actually mean is "The Wall," a graffitied ledge sitting directly above the Caldecott Tunnel.

properties.name: Guadalupe Dunes & the Lost City of DeMille
properties.hook: Cecil B. DeMille built the largest movie set in history out here in 1923, then had the whole Egyptian city torn down and secretly buried in the dunes, where it still is.

properties.name: Hakone Gardens
properties.hook: One of the oldest Japanese-style residential gardens in the Western Hemisphere, terraced into a Saratoga hillside since 1915 — a pocket of engineered calm above Silicon Valley.

properties.name: Half Moon Bay
properties.hook: The coast's main town: a walkable old Main Street, a long state beach, and the harbor where the Mavericks legend lives.

properties.name: Hangar One
properties.hook: One of the largest freestanding buildings ever raised, an eight-acre airship hangar so big fog forms inside near the ceiling, now stripped to a bare steel skeleton.

properties.name: Harmony (pop. 18)
properties.hook: A whole town of eighteen people sits just off Highway 1, named Harmony as a peace treaty, where Hearst used to stop for buttermilk on his way to the castle.

properties.name: The Hayward Fault Walk
properties.hook: Downtown Hayward's sidewalks are slowly tearing themselves apart in real time — the Hayward Fault creeps right through town, and you can walk from crack to offset curb like a geologic crime scene.

properties.name: Hearst Castle & the San Simeon Zebras
properties.hook: A newspaper baron's marble hilltop palace, and the zebras from his private zoo still grazing the ranch below it, for free.

properties.name: Heinold's First and Last Chance
properties.hook: An 1884 saloon built from a whaling ship's timbers, where the 1906 earthquake tilted the floor so steeply your beer sits crooked — and where a teenage Jack London did his homework at the bar.

properties.name: Hendy Woods & the Hermit
properties.hook: Two groves of thousand-year-old redwoods sit in the middle of Anderson Valley's wine country, and a Russian hermit once lived among them in shelters he built from fallen wood.

properties.name: Henry W. Coe State Park
properties.hook: The largest state park in Northern California, 87,000 acres of empty ridgelines an hour from San Jose, and almost nobody goes.

properties.name: Henry Cowell Redwoods & the Garden of Eden
properties.hook: Old-growth redwoods you can walk in twenty flat minutes — and downstream, the San Lorenzo River pools into genuine swimming holes, including the worst-kept secret in the Santa Cruz Mountains, the Garden of Eden.

properties.name: Henry Miller Memorial Library
properties.hook: A bookstore with no sign, free coffee, a ping-pong table, and a lawn where Arcade Fire has played.

properties.name: Hetch Hetchy
properties.hook: Yosemite has a twin — a second glacier-carved valley John Muir called a "wonderfully exact counterpart" — and San Francisco drowned it. What's left is the park's emptiest grand landscape.

properties.name: Hidden Falls Regional Park
properties.hook: Twelve hundred acres of foothill oak woodland north of Auburn, laced with thirty miles of trail and a pair of creek waterfalls with decks built out over them.

properties.name: Hidden Villa
properties.hook: A working organic farm in the Los Altos hills that opened the first youth hostel in the American West, and still rents you a bunk.

properties.name: Hiller Aviation Museum
properties.hook: A hangar of oddball aircraft at the little San Carlos airport, from a walk-in 747 nose to the flying cars that never were.

properties.name: Hole-in-the-Wall Beach (Davenport)
properties.hook: A sea arch punched through a sandstone cliff on the Davenport coast — walk through the hole at low tide and a second, emptier beach opens on the other side.

properties.name: Hornitos
properties.hook: A Mexican-founded gold town that outlived its neighbors and then just stopped — iron-doored ruins around an empty plaza, including the store where Domingo Ghirardelli sold chocolate before San Francisco.

properties.name: Hot Creek Geological Site
properties.hook: A trout stream runs straight through the floor of a live volcanic caldera — boiling turquoise pools erupt in the creekbed itself, steam pouring off snowmelt water.

properties.name: Ina Coolbrith Park
properties.hook: A vertical pocket garden stacked into the Russian Hill stairways with one of the city's finest bay views — named for California's first poet laureate, who mentored Jack London from this hill.

properties.name: The Indian Canyons
properties.hook: The largest palm oasis on Earth is a fifteen-mile canyon of native fan palms along a desert stream, on Cahuilla land just south of Palm Springs.

properties.name: Chaw'se Indian Grinding Rock
properties.hook: A slab of limestone in the Sierra foothills is dimpled with 1,185 grinding holes, the most of any bedrock mortar site in North America, worn there by the Miwok over thousands of years.

properties.name: Indian Rock Park
properties.hook: A volcanic rock the size of a building sits in the middle of a Berkeley neighborhood, with staircases carved into its face and the entire Golden Gate framed from the top.

properties.name: The Inkwells & Lagunitas Creek
properties.hook: A set of dark rock pools on Lagunitas Creek that flips identity twice a year: swimming hole in summer, and in winter one of the last places on the coast to watch wild coho salmon leap.

properties.name: The Integratron
properties.hook: A man said aliens from Venus gave him the plans for a machine to reverse aging, and he spent eighteen years building it in the desert; today people lie on the floor inside it for sound baths.

properties.name: The Inverness Shipwreck (SS Point Reyes)
properties.hook: A wooden fishing boat that beached itself on the Tomales Bay mudflats decades ago and never left — listing in the reeds, weathered silver, one of the most photographed wrecks in California.

properties.name: Jepson Prairie
properties.hook: For a few weeks each spring, one of California's last great vernal-pool prairies performs its trick: as the seasonal lakes evaporate, native wildflowers bloom outward in concentric rings of yellow, white, and blue chasing the shrinking water.

properties.name: Joshua Tree National Park
properties.hook: The Joshua trees only grow where the Mojave meets the Colorado desert, and this is the park built around that line: eight hundred thousand acres of them and the granite climbers come from everywhere to touch.

properties.name: Jughandle Ecological Staircase
properties.hook: A single trail near Mendocino climbs a "staircase" of five sea terraces — each one about 100,000 years older than the one below it — so you walk from young coastal prairie up half a million years to a forest of full-grown trees stunted to waist height.

properties.name: Kelso Dunes
properties.hook: Climb these Mojave dunes, slide back down, and the whole slope answers with a low booming hum you can feel through your feet.

properties.name: Kernville & the Kern River
properties.hook: The Kern River drops out of the high Sierra in one of the wildest whitewater runs in the country, and the old mining town of Kernville sits right on the bank of it.

properties.name: Ken Kesey's La Honda
properties.hook: A log cabin in the redwoods off Highway 84 where the 1960s more or less caught fire: Ken Kesey's place, where the Acid Tests started and the Furthur bus got its paint.

properties.name: Kings Canyon & Cedar Grove
properties.hook: A glacial gorge deeper than the Grand Canyon runs into the southern Sierra, and John Muir thought it beat Yosemite.

properties.name: Kirby Cove
properties.hook: A hidden cove tucked directly beneath the Marin end of the Golden Gate Bridge — a coarse-sand beach framed by cypress and a WWII gun battery, where you look up at the bridge's underside and out at San Francisco across the water.

properties.name: Knights Ferry Covered Bridge
properties.hook: The longest covered bridge west of the Mississippi crosses the Stanislaus at a gold-rush town most people have never heard of.

properties.name: Lake Merritt & Children's Fairyland
properties.hook: America's first official wildlife refuge (1870) is a saltwater lagoon in downtown Oakland — ringed by a light necklace, patrolled by pelicans, crossed by Venetian gondolas, and home to the storybook park that inspired Disneyland.

properties.name: Lands End & the Sutro Baths Ruins
properties.hook: The ruins of the largest swimming pool complex ever built, crumbling into the sea at the wildest corner of the city.

properties.name: Lassen & Bumpass Hell
properties.hook: California has its own Yellowstone and almost nobody goes: a plug-dome volcano that erupted in 1915, with a boiling basin named for the guide who fell through the crust and lost a leg showing it off.

properties.name: Lava Beds National Monument
properties.hook: A volcanic tableland in California's far northeast is riddled with more than 700 lava-tube caves you explore by headlamp — and one of them, a natural lava fortress, is where 53 Modoc fighters held off ten times their number for months.

properties.name: Lick Observatory (Mt Hamilton)
properties.hook: The world's first permanently staffed mountaintop observatory crowns the Bay Area's highest peak — and James Lick, the eccentric millionaire who paid for it, is buried under the telescope.

properties.name: Limekiln State Park
properties.hook: The park most people blow past on the way to somewhere more famous, and the one a few of them come back raving about.

properties.name: The Pacifica Taco Bell
properties.hook: The only Taco Bell with a walk-up window for surfers and a beach out the back door.

properties.name: Little Lakes Valley
properties.hook: The most alpine lakes you can reach for the least climbing in the whole Sierra: a nearly flat trail from the highest trailhead in the range, past lake after lake under a wall of thirteen-thousand-foot peaks.

properties.name: Little Yosemite (Sunol Wilderness)
properties.hook: A boulder-choked gorge on Alameda Creek in the Sunol backcountry that early park boosters named "Little Yosemite" — overselling it charmingly, and it's still worth the walk.

properties.name: Locke & the Delta
properties.hook: The only town in America built by Chinese immigrants for Chinese immigrants still stands on a Delta levee — one wooden block of leaning false-front buildings, half museum, half lived-in.

properties.name: The Lone Cypress & 17-Mile Drive
properties.hook: A solitary cypress has clung to a bare granite headland over Carmel Bay for some 250 years — one of the most photographed trees in North America — anchoring a private coastal drive that's been a scenic pilgrimage since the 1880s.

properties.name: Los Rios Historic District
properties.hook: The oldest neighborhood in California is a short, shaded street of adobe houses across the tracks from the San Juan Capistrano mission, and people still live in them.

properties.name: Los Trancos San Andreas Fault Trail
properties.hook: A short loop that walks you straight down the San Andreas Fault, past a fence the 1906 earthquake shoved out of line.

properties.name: The Lost Coast (King Range)
properties.hook: The mountains here rise 4,000 feet straight out of the surf in under three miles — so steep and unstable that highway engineers gave up and routed Highway 1 inland, leaving the longest roadless coastline in the Lower 48.

properties.name: Ganna Walska Lotusland
properties.hook: A Polish opera singer married six times spent forty years turning a Montecito estate into nineteen theatrical gardens, and paid for the rarest one by auctioning off her jewels.

properties.name: The Madonna Inn
properties.hook: A highway builder opened a hotel in 1958, painted nearly all of it pink, gave each of its 110 rooms a different over-the-top theme, and put a rock waterfall in the men's urinal.

properties.name: Manzanar National Historic Site
properties.hook: Between the highway and the Sierra wall, the government held eleven thousand of its own citizens behind barbed wire for being of Japanese descent.

properties.name: Mare Island
properties.hook: The US Navy's first Pacific shipyard — 142 years of warships, from wooden paddle steamers to nuclear submarines — sits half-preserved, half-reborn across the strait from Vallejo, with a chapel full of Tiffany windows.

properties.name: Marin County Civic Center
properties.hook: Frank Lloyd Wright's last great building, a long pink-and-blue arcade draped across three hills, that Hollywood keeps casting as the future.

properties.name: The Marin Headlands
properties.hook: The most photographed view of the Golden Gate Bridge, and behind it a whole wild headland of old gun batteries, a hidden lighthouse, and a black-sand beach.

properties.name: Mavericks & Pillar Point
properties.hook: The wave a local surfed alone for fifteen years before the world found out, and one of the biggest breaks on earth.

properties.name: McCloud Falls & Castle Crags
properties.hook: Three waterfalls stacked along two miles of one river under Mount Shasta — the middle one a fifty-foot curtain into a swimming hole locals treat as the far north's best cold plunge.

properties.name: John McLaren Park
properties.hook: San Francisco's second-largest park — grasslands, forest, an amphitheater, and a sky-blue water tower with one of the city's best unknown views — used by almost nobody but its neighbors.

properties.name: McWay Falls
properties.hook: An 80-foot waterfall that drops straight onto a beach you're not allowed to walk on.

properties.name: Memorial Park (Pescadero Creek)
properties.hook: San Mateo County's oldest park: a grove of genuine old-growth redwoods on Pescadero Creek with a summer swimming hole, deep in the coast-side hills.

properties.name: Mendocino
properties.hook: A New England village on a California headland — saltbox houses and redwood water towers on a bluff wrapped on three sides by state-park coast, at the far edge of the three-hour radius.

properties.name: The Methuselah Tree
properties.hook: An 1,800-year-old redwood stands right at the edge of Skyline Boulevard, the biggest and oldest tree in these mountains outside Big Basin, left alone because it was too gnarled to cut.

properties.name: The Mission
properties.hook: The one part of San Francisco to walk with no plan. It's the warmest, densest, most argued-over square mile in the city.

properties.name: The Mission Inn
properties.hook: A hotel keeper spent fifty-five years turning a small adobe boarding house into a full-block fantasia of towers, flying buttresses, and catacombs, and it's still a working hotel.

properties.name: Mission San Antonio de Padua
properties.hook: Of all twenty-one California missions, only this one still stands alone in its original landscape — no town grew around it, because the Army bought the entire valley, and Hearst owned it before them.

properties.name: Modesto & the American Graffiti Cruise
properties.hook: The cruising culture George Lucas grew up in became American Graffiti, and Modesto still cruises McHenry Avenue every summer to prove the movie right.

properties.name: Mono Lake
properties.hook: One of the oldest lakes on the continent — at least 760,000 years in its basin — twice as salty as the ocean, ringed by limestone towers that grew underwater — drained nearly to death by Los Angeles and saved by a lawsuit that rewrote California water law.

properties.name: Montaña de Oro State Park
properties.hook: One of California's largest and least-tamed state parks: eight thousand acres of golden cliffs, sea caves, and blowholes just south of Morro Bay.

properties.name: Point Montara Lighthouse Hostel
properties.hook: A working lighthouse where the old keepers' quarters are now cheap bunks a few feet from the surf.

properties.name: Monterey Bay Aquarium
properties.hook: Built inside a defunct sardine cannery on Steinbeck's Cannery Row, this is the aquarium that first grew a living kelp forest indoors — a three-story, sunlit tank of giant kelp swaying in real surge — and it's widely rated among the best in the world.

properties.name: Montgomery Woods
properties.hook: An old-growth redwood grove that briefly held the world's tallest known tree — reached by a mountain road so out-of-the-way that you'll likely have it entirely to yourself.

properties.name: Moonstone Beach (Cambria)
properties.hook: A bluff-top boardwalk where you hunt for moonstones, watch otters, and drop into tide pools, all in an easy mile and a half.

properties.name: Morgan Territory
properties.hook: The emptiest of the East Bay's big parklands, on Mount Diablo's hidden back side — reached by a one-lane mountain road that locals describe with equal parts love and horror.

properties.name: Mori Point
properties.hook: A dramatic Pacifica headland of cliffs and hidden ponds, a short walk off the highway, that most people speeding through never stop for.

properties.name: Morro Rock
properties.hook: A 581-foot volcanic plug rises straight out of the water at the harbor mouth, sacred to two tribes and closed to climbers, with peregrines nesting on its face.

properties.name: Moss Beach Distillery
properties.hook: A Prohibition speakeasy on a bluff over the ocean, still supposedly haunted by the jilted "Blue Lady," Northern California's most famous ghost.

properties.name: Mount Davidson & the Cross
properties.hook: The highest natural point in San Francisco is a eucalyptus-forested peak most residents can't name, topped by a 103-foot concrete cross with a strange civic history.

properties.name: Mount Diablo Summit
properties.hook: On the right day you can stand on this East Bay summit and see the Farallon Islands out one side and Half Dome out the other — one of the longest sightlines in the country.

properties.name: Mount Madonna
properties.hook: A redwood mountain between Gilroy and the sea, where white deer descended from a Hearst gift graze beside the ruins of a cattle king's summer mansion.

properties.name: Mount Shasta
properties.hook: A 14,179-foot glaciered volcano stands alone above the far-north forests, visible for a hundred miles — sacred to the Wintu and Karuk, a magnet for mountaineers and mystics alike, with an alpine spring-meadow you can walk to near the timberline.

properties.name: Mount St. Helena & the Silverado Squat
properties.hook: Robert Louis Stevenson spent his honeymoon squatting in an abandoned mine bunkhouse on this mountain above Calistoga — and the 4,343-foot summit he climbed still gives, on the right day, a view from the Pacific to Mount Shasta.

properties.name: Mount Sutro Cloud Forest
properties.hook: A hundred-year-old eucalyptus cloud forest hidden in the dead center of San Francisco, best on the exact foggy days everyone else avoids.

properties.name: Mount Tamalpais
properties.hook: The mountain the whole Bay Area looks up at, with a fern-and-redwood canyon of wooden ladders on one side and the open ocean on the other.

properties.name: Mount Umunhum
properties.hook: A Santa Cruz Mountains summit crowned by a five-story concrete Cold War radar cube, closed to the public for 60 years and only reopened in 2017.

properties.name: Mountain View Cemetery (Oakland)
properties.hook: Frederick Law Olmsted — the man who designed Central Park — laid out this Oakland cemetery in 1863, and locals treat it as one of the East Bay's best walking parks, crowned by a row of robber-baron pyramids with a Golden Gate view.

properties.name: Mount Hamilton Road (Hwy 130)
properties.hook: Nineteen miles and roughly 365 turns, graded gently enough for horse-drawn telescope wagons in 1876 — which is exactly why cyclists and drivers treat it as the Bay Area's great ceremonial climb.

properties.name: Marin Museum of Bicycling & MTB Hall of Fame
properties.hook: A Fairfax museum on the exact ground where mountain biking was invented, home to the Mountain Bike Hall of Fame.

properties.name: Muir Woods & Muir Beach
properties.hook: The most famous old-growth redwoods near the city, awe-inspiring and reservation-only crowded, with a quieter beach and cliff overlook just down the hill.

properties.name: Musée Mécanique
properties.hook: One of the world's largest private collections of coin-operated antique arcade machines — a century of penny arcades, still working, still a quarter — hiding in plain sight on Fisherman's Wharf.

properties.name: The Museum of Jurassic Technology
properties.hook: A small museum on a Los Angeles boulevard shows you an ant with a fungus bursting from its head, a sculpture carved to sit inside the eye of a needle, and a dozen exhibits that may or may not be true, and it won't tell you which.

properties.name: Mussel Rock
properties.hook: The exact spot where the San Andreas Fault dives into the Pacific, now a hang-gliding launch perched on top of an old landfill.

properties.name: The Mystery Spot
properties.hook: A tilted shack in the redwoods where balls roll uphill and people lean past falling — a 1940s roadside "gravitational anomaly" that is, wonderfully, a franchise built from shared blueprints.

properties.name: Natural Bridges & West Cliff
properties.hook: Each winter thousands of monarch butterflies hang in dripping orange clusters in a eucalyptus grove behind the beach — beside a surviving sea arch and the clifftop walk past the best surf-watching perch in California.

properties.name: Nepenthe
properties.hook: A clifftop terrace with a fifty-mile view that Orson Welles bought on a whim and never once slept in.

properties.name: Nevada City & the South Yuba
properties.hook: The best-preserved Gold Rush town in the Sierra foothills, all gas lamps and balconied Victorians — with the emerald swimming holes of the South Yuba River ten minutes away.

properties.name: New Almaden & Quicksilver Park
properties.hook: The Gold Rush ran on mercury, and the mercury came from here: California's oldest and richest mine, with its company town still intact in a San Jose canyon.

properties.name: New Camaldoli Hermitage
properties.hook: A working Benedictine monastery on a ridge two miles up a private road, where you can buy honey the monks made and just sit with the quiet.

properties.name: Nike Missile Site SF-51
properties.hook: The abandoned concrete bones of a Cold War missile base on a Pacifica ridge, now a windswept graffiti gallery.

properties.name: Nike Missile Site SF-88
properties.hook: The only fully restored Nike missile base in the country, where docents raise an actual Cold War nuclear missile out of its silo for you.

properties.name: Niles (Fremont)
properties.hook: Before Hollywood, there was Niles: the small-town main street where Charlie Chaplin filmed "The Tramp," with a silent-film museum that still screens movies the old way and a steam railroad running up the canyon.

properties.name: Forest of Nisene Marks
properties.hook: The epicenter of the earthquake that stopped the 1989 World Series is a small sign in the woods, and you can hike to it.

properties.name: Nitt Witt Ridge
properties.hook: The poor man's Hearst Castle: a hillside palace one man built by hand over fifty years out of beer cans, abalone shells, and whatever the town threw away.

properties.name: Noah Purifoy Desert Art Museum
properties.hook: An artist spent the last fifteen years of his life turning ten acres of open desert into a walk-through city of sculptures built from junk, and left it unfenced on purpose.

properties.name: California Scenario (Noguchi Garden)
properties.hook: One of the country's best sculpture gardens is a free acre and a half of desert, forest, and stone tucked between two office towers in Costa Mesa.

properties.name: North Beach
properties.hook: The old Italian quarter where the Beats set up shop, and still the best neighborhood in the city to wander on caffeine and no plan.

properties.name: North Table Mountain & Phantom Falls
properties.hook: For a few weeks each spring, a lava mesa above Oroville turns solid wildflower and pours waterfalls off its cliffs; by summer both are gone.

properties.name: Ojai & Bart's Books
properties.hook: A valley town under the Topatopa Mountains lights up pink at sunset, and its bookstore is a hundred and thirty thousand books shelved outdoors on the sidewalk, sold on the honor system.

properties.name: Old Faithful Geyser of California
properties.hook: One of the few geysers on Earth that erupts on a reliable clock, every few minutes, in a Calistoga field shared with fainting goats.

properties.name: Old Haul Road
properties.hook: A gentle gravel rail-trail through the redwoods on the bed of an old logging railroad, which most Bay Area riders never find.

properties.name: The Old LA Zoo
properties.hook: The original Los Angeles Zoo closed in 1966 and was just left there, so now you can climb through the empty bear grottos and picnic inside the old cages, free, in the middle of Griffith Park.

properties.name: The Old Sacramento Underground
properties.hook: After floods kept drowning it, Sacramento jacked its whole downtown up a full story in the 1860s, and you can still tour the streets and storefronts it buried.

properties.name: Old Shasta (Shasta State Historic Park)
properties.hook: A half-mile of roofless brick storefronts with iron doors is nearly all that's left of the town that was once the capital of California's northern gold mines.

properties.name: Pacific Grove
properties.hook: The Victorian beach town on the tip of the peninsula that locals quietly rate over Carmel, minus the crowds and the price tags.

properties.name: Palace of Fine Arts
properties.hook: A monumental Roman rotunda reflected in a lagoon, built as a fake ruin for a 1915 world's fair and the only building from it left standing.

properties.name: Palo Alto Baylands
properties.hook: The largest undisturbed marsh left on San Francisco Bay, flat and free at the edge of Palo Alto, laced with boardwalks and birds.

properties.name: Partington Cove
properties.hook: An unmarked trail off a blind pullout that drops through a rock tunnel to a cove most people driving past never know is there.

properties.name: Pescadero
properties.hook: A two-street farm town in the fog-and-redwood country where people drive an hour for artichoke soup and warm garlic bread.

properties.name: The Petrified Forest
properties.hook: Giant redwoods turned entirely to stone by a volcanic eruption three million years ago, lying where they fell, near Calistoga.

properties.name: The Pez Museum
properties.hook: A little Burlingame storefront holding the world's largest collection of Pez dispensers, plus a museum of toys that got banned.

properties.name: Pfeiffer Beach
properties.hook: Purple sand and an offshore rock with a keyhole the winter sun fires straight through, reached by a road with no sign.

properties.name: Pfeiffer Falls & Valley View
properties.hook: A short redwood loop to a 60-foot fall, the easy leg-stretch inside the park most people only drive through to reach Pfeiffer Beach.

properties.name: Picchetti Ranch & Winery
properties.hook: One of the oldest surviving wineries in California still stands on the Monte Bello ridge above Cupertino — an 1870s Italian homestead where peacocks strut the grounds and you can taste wine on the porch of a building older than the state's wine industry.

properties.name: Piedras Blancas Elephant Seal Rookery
properties.hook: Thousands of elephant seals sprawled on a beach a few feet below a boardwalk, for free, any day of the year.

properties.name: Pigeon Point Lighthouse
properties.hook: One of the tallest lighthouses on the West Coast, with a hostel in the keepers' houses and a hot tub bolted to the edge of the cliff.

properties.name: Pinnacles National Park
properties.hook: The Bay Area's own national park is the one nobody visits: half of an ancient volcano that drifted 195 miles up the San Andreas Fault, full of caves you scramble through and condors overhead.

properties.name: Pioneertown & Pappy and Harriet's
properties.hook: In 1946 a group of Western movie stars built a fake 1880s town in the high desert to film in and live in, and it's still standing, still a real town, with a roadhouse out back that books serious music.

properties.name: Papua New Guinea Sculpture Garden
properties.hook: A quiet grove near Stanford full of towering carved totems and a dugout canoe, made by New Guinea artists flown in to carve the wood on site.

properties.name: Point Arena & the Stornetta Lands
properties.hook: The tallest lighthouse on the Pacific coast stands where the San Andreas Fault runs out to sea — beside miles of public bluffs pocked with sinkholes, sea arches, and blowholes you wander with no fences and no crowds.

properties.name: Point Bonita Lighthouse
properties.hook: The only lighthouse in America you reach by crossing a suspension bridge — out a hand-carved rock tunnel and over a miniature Golden Gate to a light clinging to the last crag of the Marin Headlands, where the Bay meets the ocean.

properties.name: Point Isabel
properties.hook: One of the largest off-leash dog parks in the country — 23 acres of Richmond bayshore where a half-million dog visits a year happen under nothing but voice control, all with the Golden Gate Bridge as the backdrop.

properties.name: Point Lobos State Natural Reserve
properties.hook: The one Big Sur stop worth setting an alarm for, and the reason to be at a gate at 8am on vacation.

properties.name: Point Reyes Lighthouse & Chimney Rock
properties.hook: The windiest, foggiest point on the West Coast: 308 steps down to a lighthouse on the cliff, and in winter a beach heaving with elephant seals over the ridge.

properties.name: Point Sur Lighthouse
properties.hook: A working lighthouse stranded on a 361-foot volcanic rock, and the only one on the coast you can walk through, on a docent's schedule.

properties.name: Poly Canyon (the Architecture Graveyard)
properties.hook: A canyon behind Cal Poly holds sixty years of student-built experimental structures — a shell house, a bridge house, geodesic domes — slowly weathering where their builders left them.

properties.name: Port Costa
properties.hook: A dead-end former grain port on the Carquinez Strait that time forgot: one street, a Victorian roadhouse, and a cavernous warehouse bar with a stuffed polar bear.

properties.name: Portola Redwoods State Park
properties.hook: A deep, quiet redwood canyon at the far side of the Skyline hills that takes real effort to reach, so almost nobody bothers.

properties.name: Preston Castle
properties.hook: A 120-room Romanesque castle rises over the Gold Country town of Ione — built in 1894 not as a mansion but as a reform school, and abandoned with a reputation dark enough that ghost-hunting TV keeps coming back.

properties.name: Museum of International Propaganda
properties.hook: A small, free San Rafael museum of a century's worth of state propaganda, one couple's unsettling collection of posters, cartoons, and personality-cult kitsch.

properties.name: Pulgas Water Temple
properties.hook: A Roman-style temple hidden behind a gate on a country road, built in 1934 to celebrate water arriving from 160 miles away.

properties.name: Purisima Creek Redwoods
properties.hook: A deep redwood canyon on the coast side of Skyline that most people blow past on the way to the beach, with trails from flat creek walks to a lung-busting climb.

properties.name: The Rae Lakes Loop
properties.hook: A forty-mile loop out of Kings Canyon climbs to a chain of alpine lakes under a spire called the Painted Lady, over a twelve-thousand-foot pass, on some of the best trail in the Sierra.

properties.name: Ragged Point & Black Swift Falls
properties.hook: The cliff where Big Sur begins or ends, with a brutal little trail down to a black-sand beach and a 300-foot waterfall you can't see from the top.

properties.name: Rancho Corral de Tierra
properties.hook: Thousands of acres of wild coastal hills rising straight up behind the Highway 1 towns, a big empty backcountry hiding in plain sight above Montara.

properties.name: Rancho San Antonio & Deer Hollow Farm
properties.hook: The most-loved trail network on the Peninsula hides a 160-year-old working homestead in its folds — a real farm with pigs, goats, and a cow, a mile's flat walk from a Cupertino parking lot, at the foot of 25 miles of hills.

properties.name: Reinhardt Redwood Regional Park
properties.hook: Ships entering the Golden Gate once navigated by redwoods on this Oakland ridge so huge they were visible 16 miles out at sea — all cut by 1860, and the forest that grew back is now the East Bay's secret cathedral.

properties.name: Ring Mountain
properties.hook: A Tiburon ridge with 2,400-year-old Native carvings pecked into the boulders, a wildflower found nowhere else on Earth, and a full sweep of the bay.

properties.name: Roaring Camp Railroads
properties.hook: Century-old steam locomotives haul you up some of the steepest railroad grades in North America, straight through old-growth redwoods — on engines dating to 1890, among the oldest authentically preserved narrow-gauge steam still running regular passenger service in America.

properties.name: Rodin Sculpture Garden (Cantor)
properties.hook: Rodin's Gates of Hell, and the largest collection of his bronzes outside Paris, sitting free and outdoors on the Stanford campus.

properties.name: Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum
properties.hook: The largest collection of Egyptian artifacts on the US West Coast — real mummies included — built and run by a mystical order in a San Jose neighborhood, in a campus styled after an Egyptian temple complex.

properties.name: Rosie the Riveter NHP (Richmond)
properties.hook: Richmond built 747 ships in four years — a Liberty ship in under five days at the record — and the national park telling that story sits in the very shipyards, with one of the last Victory ships still afloat at the dock.

properties.name: Russian Ridge & Borel Hill
properties.hook: The highest named point in San Mateo County sits on an open grass ridge above Skyline — and in April it detonates into one of the best wildflower displays on the Peninsula, poppies and lupine to the horizon, with the ocean on one side and the whole bay on the other.

properties.name: Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge
properties.hook: On winter afternoons a cloud of snow geese lifts off a Central Valley marsh all at once, loud enough to feel, and you can watch it from a levee road.

properties.name: Safari West
properties.hook: Four hundred acres of Sonoma hills run as an African savanna — giraffes, rhinos, cheetahs — with luxury safari tents imported from Botswana, and a fire story that tells you everything about the place.

properties.name: The Salk Institute
properties.hook: Louis Kahn's most famous building is a bare travertine courtyard split by a single thin line of water that runs straight out to the Pacific.

properties.name: Salmon Creek Falls
properties.hook: A 120-foot waterfall a five-minute walk from a highway pullout at the far south end, where almost nobody bothers to stop.

properties.name: Salt Point State Park
properties.hook: Six thousand acres of Sonoma coast where the sandstone bluffs are eaten into honeycomb lacework, one of California's first underwater parks lies just offshore, and the ridgetop forest is a garden of full-grown trees stunted to knee height.

properties.name: The Salton Sea & Bombay Beach
properties.hook: California's largest lake was an accident, a river breach that flooded the desert in 1905, and its dead resort towns are now the eeriest landscape in the state.

properties.name: Salvation Mountain
properties.hook: One man spent thirty years painting a hill in the desert, with half a million gallons of paint, to say as big as he could that God is love.

properties.name: The Samoa Cookhouse
properties.hook: The last lumber-camp cookhouse in the West still feeds you the way it fed loggers in 1893: family-style at long tables, in bowls you pass around, until nobody leaves hungry.

properties.name: Sam's Castle
properties.hook: An actual stone castle on a Pacifica hillside, built after 1906 by a man who wanted to be earthquake-proof, with a wild century behind it.

properties.name: San Bruno Mountain
properties.hook: A wild mountain marooned in the middle of the urban peninsula, with rare butterflies and a summit view from the ocean to the bay to the Farallones.

properties.name: San Carpóforo Beach
properties.hook: The wide, driftwood-strewn beach at the very bottom of Big Sur that almost nobody stops for, because there's no sign telling them to.

properties.name: San Gregorio General Store
properties.hook: An 1889 general store that's really a saloon with live music, stiff Bloody Marys, and shelves of books, boots, and cast iron.

properties.name: San Jose Flea Market
properties.hook: "La Pulga" — one of the largest open-air markets in America since 1960 — is eight lanes of produce rows, taco smoke, quinceañera dresses, and live banda that most of the Bay Area north of it has never visited.

properties.name: San Pedro Valley & Brooks Falls
properties.hook: Twenty-five minutes from San Francisco, a three-tier waterfall drops 175 feet down a Pacifica canyon each winter — and most of the city has never heard of it.

properties.name: Sand Dollar Beach & Jade Cove
properties.hook: The one place on 90 miles of cliff coast where you can walk a long, open beach, and dig real jade out of the surf next door.

properties.name: Santa Barbara County Courthouse
properties.hook: A working county courthouse is one of the most beautiful public buildings in the country, and you can ride an elevator up its clock tower for a free view over the whole city to the sea.

properties.name: The Sausalito Houseboats
properties.hook: A floating neighborhood of wildly individual houseboats on the Sausalito waterfront, the mellow, arty remnant of a 1960s bohemia.

properties.name: Sawyer Camp Trail & Crystal Springs
properties.hook: A paved car-free trail running straight down the San Andreas Fault, along two reservoirs, past a laurel tree older than California.

properties.name: Charles M. Schulz Museum
properties.hook: Charles Schulz drew Peanuts in Santa Rosa for thirty years — and the museum across from his beloved ice rink holds the drawn-by-hand evidence of the most widely read comic strip in history.

properties.name: Sea Ranch Chapel
properties.hook: A tiny, wave-shaped chapel on the Sonoma coast that looks like a seashell grew out of the meadow, open to anyone, of any faith or none.

properties.name: The Junípero Serra Statue
properties.hook: A 26-foot concrete Junípero Serra kneeling by the freeway and pointing west, that locals swear looks sculpted from mashed potatoes.

properties.name: The Seward Street Slides
properties.hook: Two-story concrete slides built into one of the city's steepest hills, designed by a fourteen-year-old, ridden on a scrap of cardboard.

properties.name: Sibley Volcanic Preserve
properties.hook: A ten-million-year-old volcano lies in the Oakland hills — tipped on its side by the Hayward Fault so you can walk straight into its guts — and someone has laid stone labyrinths in the old quarry pits at the bottom.

properties.name: Sign Hill
properties.hook: A hillside spelling out "SOUTH SAN FRANCISCO THE INDUSTRIAL CITY" in giant concrete letters, with a trail that climbs up through them.

properties.name: The Skunk Train
properties.hook: A logging railroad from 1885 still climbs off the Mendocino coast into the redwoods, and you can ride it in a rail motorcar or pedal it yourself on an electric railbike.

properties.name: SLAC (the two-mile accelerator)
properties.hook: A particle accelerator two miles long running under Interstate 280, once the longest building in the world, where they smashed atoms into Nobel Prizes.

properties.name: Spruce Street Suspension Bridge
properties.hook: A wooden footbridge from 1912 hangs on steel cables over a wooded canyon in the middle of San Diego, and it sways when you walk across it.

properties.name: SS Palo Alto (the Cement Ship)
properties.hook: A concrete ocean tanker — built when WWI steel ran short — has been beached at the end of the Seacliff pier since 1930, spending its century as a dance-hall, a fishing spot, and now a crumbling seabird reef.

properties.name: Stinson Beach
properties.hook: The Bay Area's classic beach day: a long arc of sand under Mount Tam, water warm enough to actually swim on a good day, and a trailhead for a legendary hike.

properties.name: Sue-meg State Park
properties.hook: The first California park to get its name back: Sue-meg, as the Yurok have always called this headland, with a living village site and a beach where people hunt agates.

properties.name: Sugarloaf Ridge & the Ferguson Observatory
properties.hook: A state park in the Sonoma hills holds the largest observatory in the West built purely for the public, under some of the darkest skies near the Bay.

properties.name: Sundial Bridge
properties.hook: A white spar leans over the Sacramento River in Redding, holding up a glass-decked footbridge that is also a working sundial, one of the largest in the world.

properties.name: Sunken City
properties.hook: A cliffside neighborhood in San Pedro slid into the ocean starting in 1929, and the broken streets and foundations are still out there on the bluff, tilted and graffitied, behind a fence people keep breaking.

properties.name: Sunny Jim's Sea Cave
properties.hook: The only sea cave in California you can reach on foot is at the bottom of a hand-dug tunnel that starts inside a La Jolla gift shop.

properties.name: Sutro Heights Park
properties.hook: A silver baron built a clifftop estate above the Cliff House, filled its gardens with two hundred statues, and opened it to the public for a dime; the mansion is gone but the parapet and the lions remain.

properties.name: Sutter Buttes
properties.hook: A ring of eroded volcanic domes rises 2,000 feet straight out of the flat Sacramento Valley — often called the world's smallest mountain range, a whole extinct volcano you can drive around in twenty minutes but almost never enter.

properties.name: Sutter's Fort
properties.hook: The adobe fort a Swiss immigrant built in 1839 became the seed the whole city of Sacramento grew around, and it's still standing in the middle of it.

properties.name: Swami's & the Self-Realization Fellowship
properties.hook: A world-famous surf break named itself after the swami whose ashram sits on the bluff above it, and the ashram's meditation garden is open, quiet, and free.

properties.name: Sweeney Ridge & the Bay Discovery Site
properties.hook: The ridge where a lost Spanish expedition first laid eyes on San Francisco Bay, now a windswept hike with the whole thing at your feet.

properties.name: Taft Point & Sentinel Dome
properties.hook: One trailhead on Glacier Point Road serves both of Yosemite's best cheap thrills: a guardrail-free ledge hanging 3,000 feet over the Valley, and a bald dome with a 360° view that embarrasses Glacier Point itself.

properties.name: Tahoe East Shore Coves
properties.hook: The Nevada side's granite-boulder coves are the Caribbean of the Sierra — and between the famous beach and the nude one runs a string of pocket coves most visitors never find.

properties.name: Tassajara Zen Mountain Center
properties.hook: A Zen monastery and clothing-optional hot springs at the end of fourteen miles of dirt road, open to outsiders four months a year.

properties.name: Tennessee Valley & Tennessee Cove
properties.hook: A flat valley walk in the Marin Headlands that ends at a black-sand cove named for a gold-rush steamship that wrecked there in 1853 — and whose engine still surfaces at extreme low tides.

properties.name: The Bike Hut
properties.hook: An unlocked garage on a farm road, stocked with snacks, coffee, and spare tubes, that runs entirely on the honor system for whoever rides by.

properties.name: The Dish
properties.hook: A giant Cold War radio telescope on a grassy hill, ringed by a paved loop that half of Palo Alto treats as its living room.

properties.name: Thousand Island Lake
properties.hook: An alpine lake scattered with rocky islets sits under Banner Peak, and it's the reflection Ansel Adams made famous.

properties.name: Tilden Park Steam Trains
properties.hook: Two separate miniature railways sit side by side in the Berkeley hills — a coal-fired steam line that hauls you through actual redwoods, and next door a hobbyist club running their own home-built locomotives.

properties.name: 16th Avenue Tiled Steps
properties.hook: A staircase in the far avenues that a whole neighborhood turned into a single sea-to-sky mosaic, and almost no visitor makes it out here.

properties.name: Tomales Bay Bioluminescence Paddle
properties.hook: On moonless summer and fall nights, Tomales Bay glows — every paddle stroke ignites blue-green fire, fish trail comet streaks, and your wake burns behind you.

properties.name: Tomales Bay Oysters
properties.hook: Shuck your own oysters at a picnic table on the edge of a quiet bay, or paddle it on a dark night when the water glows blue.

properties.name: Tomales Point & the Tule Elk Reserve
properties.hook: Elk on the tip of a peninsula, ocean on one side and Tomales Bay on the other, and usually no one else on the trail.

properties.name: Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve
properties.hook: The rarest pine in North America grows wild in only two places on Earth, and one of them is a set of eroded sea cliffs just north of La Jolla.

properties.name: The Tourist Club (Die Naturfreunde)
properties.hook: A Bavarian-style lodge hidden on the side of Mount Tam that pours cold beer on a sunny deck, but only for hikers, and only on certain Sundays.

properties.name: Trinidad
properties.hook: A fishing village of fewer than four hundred people sits over a rock-studded harbor on the far north coast, with ten beaches within a few minutes of its one main street.

properties.name: Tuolumne Meadows & the Tioga High Country
properties.hook: An hour past the Valley crowds, Tioga Road climbs into a different park entirely: subalpine meadows at 8,600 feet, glacier-polished domes, and lakes you can actually hear.

properties.name: Uvas Canyon
properties.hook: A pocket canyon behind Morgan Hill stacks half a dozen waterfalls along a single one-mile loop — the South Bay's rain-day secret, gated by a reservation system that keeps it sane.

properties.name: Vasquez Rocks
properties.hook: The tilted sandstone slabs where Captain Kirk fought the Gorn stand an hour north of LA, shoved up on end by the San Andreas Fault.

properties.name: Vicente Flat & Cone Peak
properties.hook: A backpacking route from the edge of the highway to the tallest coastal mountain in the lower 48, and a redwood camp one local calls the best on the planet.

properties.name: Victoria Beach Pirate Tower
properties.hook: A sixty-foot stone tower shaped like a castle turret stands on a Laguna Beach cove, and the tide has to be out for you to reach it.

properties.name: Villa Montalvo
properties.hook: A senator built an Italian Mediterranean villa into the Saratoga foothills in 1912, filled it with carved Granada doors and hand-worked woodwork, and left it to California — where it became one of the country's oldest artist residencies, ringed by free hiking trails.

properties.name: Waterdog Lake
properties.hook: A hidden reservoir at the top of a Belmont street, a genuine little lake tucked in the hills with trails all around it.

properties.name: The Watts Towers
properties.hook: An Italian immigrant tile-setter spent thirty-three years building seventeen steel towers in his Watts backyard out of broken glass and pottery, the tallest a hundred feet, then handed over the deed and walked away.

properties.name: The Wave Organ
properties.hook: A musical instrument you play by waiting for the tide, built out of a torn-down cemetery at the tip of a Marina jetty.

properties.name: Weaverville Joss House
properties.hook: In a gold-rush town four hours north, the oldest continuously used Chinese temple in California still burns incense at an altar carried over from China in the 1870s.

properties.name: Wilder Ranch
properties.hook: A perfectly preserved 1890s dairy ranch sits at the foot of a staircase of fault-lifted coastal terraces just north of Santa Cruz — 7,000 acres where you walk or ride out onto sea-cliff prairie and, on the way, tour a Victorian farmstead still running its water-powered machine shop.

properties.name: Winchester Mystery House
properties.hook: A rifle heiress spent 38 years building a mansion with staircases into ceilings and doors onto two-story drops — and stopped only when she died.

properties.name: The Wind Harp
properties.hook: A 92-foot steel harp on a hilltop by the bay, played by nothing but the wind.

properties.name: Windy Hill
properties.hook: Two bald grassy summits on the Skyline ridge with a full-circle view: bay and valley on one side, ridges rolling to the ocean on the other.

properties.name: Wolf House (Jack London Park)
properties.hook: The stone ruins of the dream mansion Jack London built and never got to live in, burned to the ground days before he moved in.

properties.name: The Woodside Store
properties.hook: At the bottom of Kings Mountain Road sits an 1854 general store that was also the post office, the saloon, the dentist, and the library, kept just about as it was.

properties.name: Wunderlich Park
properties.hook: A redwood-and-oak forest above Woodside, threaded with old horse-estate trails, that stays quiet while the famous parks fill up.

properties.name: Horsetail Fall "Firefall"
properties.hook: For about two weeks each February, if the water is flowing and the sky is clear, the setting sun turns a thin waterfall on El Capitan into what looks exactly like molten fire pouring down the granite.

properties.name: Yosemite Valley (done right)
properties.hook: The most famous seven square miles of granite on Earth — and it still beats the hype, if you refuse to experience it as a drive-through.
